Chapter 3 Tree dormancy This chapter is presented by Dr. Erica Fadón, a researcher at HortiBonn from 2018 to 2021. It delves into the intriguing subject of dormancy in temperate fruit trees, an area still rich with mysteries awaiting exploration. A pivotal question for those studying dormancy is, “How do trees determine the right time to flower?” While it might seem obvious that the answer is spring, the truth is far more intricate. This chapter will deepen your understanding of dormancy and provide you with essential skills in using chillR to predict flowering times, even in light of the challenges posed by global warming. 3.1 Learning goals Learn about dormancy in temperate fruit trees Be able to identify the phenological stages of a fruit tree and understand phenology data sets Describe and compare the two methodologies (empirical and statistical) to identify the chilling and forcing periods of a certain cultivar 3.2 Introduction to dormancy Tree dormancy is a state of reduced activity that occurs when environmental conditions become unfavorable, particularly during winter. This state serves as a survival strategy to protect trees from extreme temperatures, water shortages, and other stress factors. During dormancy, trees slow down or halt their growth processes to conserve energy and prevent damage. Dormancy is divided into four phases: Dormancy Establishment: triggered by photoperiod and temperature Triggered by environmental signals like day length (photoperiod) and temperature, initiating the plant’s preparation for the dormant state. Endo-Dormancy: Controlled by the plant’s internal factors, where growth remains suppressed even under favorable conditions. This phase continues until the plant has experienced enough chilling, although growth does not resume immediately. Eco-Dormancy: The plant is acclimated to its environment, showing no visible growth changes but remaining in a light dormant state. Warmer temperatures are the main factor driving this phase. Growth Resumption: Visible changes typically occur in late winter or early spring as plants quickly lose their acclimation and begin to grow. During this phase, they become more sensitive to frost, with warm temperatures promoting active growth. Plant dormancy is regulated by a range of physiological processes: 3.3 Dormancy physiology 3.4 Experimental and statistical determination of the chilling and forcing periods Dormancy presents two phases during which temperatures have opposite effects on flowering. High chill accumulation during endodormancy advances flowering dates, but similarly cool temperatures during ecodormancy may cause flowering delays. The challenge is to distinguish between these two phases, even though the tree buds appear to be in the same developmental stage throughout. The next video Dormancy determination determains whether a tree is dormant or not during winter. 3.5 Phenology record and BBCH scale Phenology is the study of periodic events in biological life cycles and how these are influenced by seasonal and interannual variations in climate. This module will involve working with phenology data sets, primarily focusing on a specific stage, usually budbreak, even though trees pass through various developmental stages during the year. These stages are typically identified by numerical codes. The following video Phenology in temperate fruit trees will cover what these numbers signify. It’s essential to understand these codes to link them to visual signs, like trees covered in blossoms, trees with yellowing leaves, or trees bearing ripe, sweet fruits. 3.6 Excercises on tree dormancy 1.
